In the non-profit world, you may or may not know this, but most non-profit organizations will show you a pie chart of where your money goes, right? And X percentage goes to this, and X percentage goes to that, and X percentage goes to this, right? You may or may not know this, but the law that governs non-profits, first of all, is nowhere near as stringent as the law that governs profits, but there is some law. The law allows them to put under education, which is always on the wheel, and it's a big section. So like if you go get the arthritis foundation, the cancer foundation, or whatever, you see their pie chart, you're going to see direct services, you're going to see a patient in consumer education, and that's going to be a big hunk of the pie chart. Administration is going to be a tiny little piece of the pie chart. It allows them to put in education any direct mail or other media that has one paragraph of copy directing people to educational resources. So therefore, virtually all of the direct mail to raise money, all that expense gets put in education. It doesn't get shown on the pie chart as money being spent to raise money. So they can spend, all they got to do is put at the bottom of an eight-page fundraising letter, one paragraph that says, you know, if you have arthritis, go to arthritis.com and access these resources. And 22 million people have arthritis, and there's four things you can do about it, and there's six home remedies that work, and they're all at the website. That now qualifies the entire expense of that mailing to be shown as education, not as fundraising. So what shows up as fundraising on the chart is only the things that you can't legally show anyplace else in order to minimize, because if you show people exactly what was going on, so 72 cents out every dollar is being spent to get another dollar, and only 28 cents is left, and 14 cents of that's going to administration. See, if you reconstituted the pie chart is what it really is, and you looked at it, you think twice about continuing to give money to XYZ Foundation, because administrations like 50% of the money that's left over after the money spent getting more money. So they're allowed to show it, so the only thing that's under fundraising is like the expense for an event, so anything that can be moved over in education, so if they produce a DVD, that's all about raising money. So let's say it's about getting you to leave money to them in your will. All they've got to have on that DVD is 10% of the minutes have to be direct education about how you can help whatever the condition is that there is money for, and the entire expense of the DVD moves over into education on the pie chart, it doesn't have to be shown, it has fundraising or advertising or marketing. It allows them to show much better pie chart. That is deceptive, it's not a lie, and it's not illegal, but the perception would be very different of people giving money to these organizations if we reconstituted the pie chart and didn't have the liberty to do that. Pretty much everybody in the non-profit world is okay with all of that. In political fundraising, a lot of people, their attitudes would probably be a lot different if they knew two things about giving money to candidates. One is that when the candidate quits whatever money is left in his campaign account that he has to spend, he gets to keep at tax-free. So everybody was sort of annoyed in the last presidential election, those who supported him that Gore actually finished up and didn't spend all the money. Well that lays there now in case he decides to do something again, but eventually if he decides to do nothing, tis. And so senators, if they end in a good incumbent who nobody serious runs against them and they don't have to spend a lot of money getting reelected but they keep raising a lot of money, when it's all said and done they may have six, seven, eight million dollars unspent in their campaign account. Well that goes in their pocket. So you may or may not like that when you support a candidate. It's not that they ever, nobody ever lies about it, but they don't tell you. So is it deceptive by omission? You know, if you put that as a disclosure statement on every request for money, the other thing there is no law about second party dealing. By that I mean my campaign for the U.S. Senate can get all its printing done by a print shop that my spouse owns. By the profit margin on the printing is going into our personal account.
